title: "Direct Testimony of Juan-Pablo Velez"
subtitle: "On Behalf of the Conservation Law Foundation — RIPUC Docket No. 2545GE"

    email: jpv@switch.box
    affiliations:
      - Switchbox

    page-layout: article
    smooth-scroll: true

::: {.callout-note}
## Working document — outline for CLF review

This is a **bulleted Q&A outline** of proposed expert testimony for CLF in RIPUC Docket No. 2545GE (Rhode Island Energy's electric distribution rate case). All statistics are computed live from the analysis. Questions are at the level of detail they would be in the final testimony; answers are at the bullet-point level for CLF to review structure, argument flow, and emphasis.

**Core framing:** This testimony is a **cost allocation** case, not a rate design case. We demonstrate a large intra-class cross-subsidy, propose a new rate subclass with a correctly sized revenue requirement, and recover it through a simple flat rate. Rate design (seasonal, TOU, etc.) is secondary — any design that recovers the right revenue requirement from HP customers fixes the cross-subsidy.

**Rhetorical arc** (per testimony style guide): (1) Establish the problem → (2) Explain why it matters → (3) Present the remedy → (4) Show it works → (5) Anticipate objections → (6) Explain the methodology.
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false

import pickle
from math import ceil, floor
from pathlib import Path
from types import SimpleNamespace

v = SimpleNamespace(**pickle.loads(Path("cache/report_variables.pkl").read_bytes()))
f = SimpleNamespace(**pickle.loads(Path("cache/feeder_report_variables.pkl").read_bytes()))
c = SimpleNamespace(
    **pickle.loads(Path("cache/report_variables_cos_subclass.pkl").read_bytes()),
)
d = SimpleNamespace(**pickle.loads(Path("cache/delivery_cost_timing_vars.pkl").read_bytes()))


def dollar(x, accuracy=0):
    return f"${x:,.{accuracy}f}"


def million(x, digits=1):
    """Whole-dollar amounts as e.g. ``$1.4 million`` for narrative testimony."""
    return f"${x / 1e6:.{digits}f} million"


def dollar_m(x, digits=1):
    """Large revenue totals as ``$159.4`` (millions of dollars, default one decimal)."""
    return f"${x / 1e6:.{digits}f}"


def pct(x, accuracy=0):
    return f"{x * 100:,.{accuracy}f}%"


def _subclass_delivery_row(ns, label):
    return next(r for r in ns.testimony_subclass_delivery_rows if r["subclass"] == label)


def _hp_subclass_delivery_row(ns):
    return _subclass_delivery_row(ns, "Heat pump")


def subclass_pct_of_residential_customers(ns, label, *, accuracy=1):
    return pct(_subclass_delivery_row(ns, label)["pct_customers_display"], accuracy)


def hp_pct_of_residential_customers(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    return pct(_hp_subclass_delivery_row(ns)["pct_customers_display"], accuracy)


def hp_pct_of_residential_delivery_revenue(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    return pct(_hp_subclass_delivery_row(ns)["pct_all_revenue"], accuracy)


def hp_pct_of_total_delivery_cos(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    t = float(ns.cos_subclass_total_cos)
    if t <= 0:
        return pct(0.0, accuracy)
    return pct(float(ns.cos_default_hp_group_cos) / t, accuracy)


def hp_pct_of_total_delivery_revenue(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    t = float(ns.cos_subclass_total_delivery_rev)
    if t <= 0:
        return pct(0.0, accuracy)
    return pct(float(ns.cos_default_hp_group_rev) / t, accuracy)


def ng_delivery_rev_as_pct_of_subclass_cos(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    cos = float(ns.cos_default_ng_group_cos)
    if cos <= 0:
        return pct(0.0, accuracy)
    return pct(float(ns.cos_default_ng_group_rev) / cos, accuracy)


def df_delivery_rev_as_pct_of_subclass_cos(ns, *, accuracy=1):
    cos = float(ns.cos_default_df_group_cos)
    if cos <= 0:
        return pct(0.0, accuracy)
    return pct(float(ns.cos_default_df_group_rev) / cos, accuracy)


def comma(x, accuracy=0):
    return f"{x:,.{accuracy}f}"


def cents(x, accuracy=1):
    return f"{abs(x) * 100:,.{accuracy}f}"


def testimony_subclass_delivery_table_html(ns) -> str:
    """Narrow delivery-revenue-by-subclass table (customers, %, revenue, % of revenue)."""
    import polars as pl
    from great_tables import GT, loc, style

    from lib.great_tables import get_switchbox_gt_tab_options

    _tdf = pl.DataFrame(ns.testimony_subclass_delivery_rows)
    _tcols = [
        "n_customers_display",
        "pct_customers_display",
        "revenue_delivery",
        "pct_all_revenue",
    ]
    _gt_sub = (
        GT(_tdf, rowname_col="subclass")
        .cols_label(
            n_customers_display="Customers",
            pct_customers_display="% of customers",
            revenue_delivery="Delivery revenue",
            pct_all_revenue="% of revenue",
        )
        .fmt_number(columns=["n_customers_display"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
        .fmt_percent(columns=["pct_customers_display", "pct_all_revenue"], decimals=1)
        .fmt_currency(columns=["revenue_delivery"], decimals=0)
        .cols_align(align="center", columns=_tcols)
        .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.stub())
        .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.column_labels())
        .tab_options(**get_switchbox_gt_tab_options(table_layout="auto"))
    )
    return _gt_sub.as_raw_html()
```


language: "moves revenues toward cost-based levels", "magnitude of bill impacts do not violate gradualism", cost obligations, allocated cost of service, separate classes defined by usage characteristics


## I. Introduction and Qualifications

**Q.** Please state your name, title, employer, and business address.
<br>
**A.** Juan-Pablo Velez. Co-founder and Executive Director Switchbox. 1 Whitehall Street, 17th Floor, New York, NY 10004.

<br><br>

**Q.** Please summarize your education and work experience.
<br>
**A.** I have a BA in Sociology from the University of Chicago. I took post-graduate mathematics courses in probability theory, mathematical statistics, and vector calculus at NYU and Columbia University.

Before founding Switchbox, I was a staff machine learning engineer at Spotify, where I helped build some of the company's large-scale AI systems. Before that, I worked as data scientist for a boutique data science consultancy called Polynumeral.

I founded and have led Switchbox for the past four years, where I have authored reports covering heat pump rates, building electrification and weatherization incentive programs, green hydrogen blending, non-pipeline alternatives, the economics and grid impacts of all-electric new construction mandates, and other topics. Our reports are based on software implementations of economics and engineering models.

I have presented our work on heat pump rate design at NEEP's HEW2025 Heat Pump Summit and in front of the Illinois Commerce Commission. Our clients have included Environmental Defense Fund, NRDC, Earthjustice, Acadia Center, Green Energy Consumers Alliance, Rewiring America, and others. Our reports have been covered by numerous national and local press outlets.

<br><br>

**Q.** Have you previously testified before the Rhode Island Public Utilities Commission?
<br>
**A.** No.

**Q.** Have you previously testified before other state Public Utilities Commissions?
<br>
**A.** No. However, Switchbox's work has been submitted to regulatory proceedings in multiple states, including:

- Docket No. 25-08 docket before the Massachusetts Department of Public Utilities for the Massachusetts, on heat pump rates
- Docket No. GR23030102 before the New Jersey Board of Public Utilities, on green hydrogen blending in gas distribution
- The People's Gas Non-pipeline Alternatives workshop resulting from the Illinois Commerce Commission's order in Docket No. 24-0081
- Docket No. 24-0158 before the Illinois Commerce Commission, the state's Future of Gas proceeding
- Docket No. 18-M-0084 before the New York Public Service Commission, on the state's energy efficiency programs
- Docket No. TK before the New York Public Service Commission, on the state's implementation of All-Electric Building Act

<br><br>

**Q.** On whose behalf are you testifying in this proceeding?
<br>
**A.** I am testifying on behalf of the Conservation Law Foundation (CLF).

<br><br>

**Q.** What is the purpose of your testimony?
<br>
**A.** I demonstrate that Rhode Island Energy's current residential electric delivery rates (A-16 and A-60) create a substantial cross-subsidy within the residential customer class: from customers that heat with heat pumps to those that heat with fossil fuels.

To measure this _intra_-class cross-subsidy, I performed a cost-of-service study of residential customer subclasses. These subclasses were defined according to their heating technology and fuel—customers who heat with heat pumps, natural gas, oil/propane, electric resistance, and other. The study produced the cost-of-service for each subclass, which I then compared to the revenue RIE collected from them during the Test Year.

I find that heat pump customers overpay relative to their cost of service by `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per year, on average, whereas customers that heat with natural gas and delivered fuels underpay relative to their cost of service by an average of `{python} dollar(abs(v.natgas_mean_delivery_bat))`  and `{python} dollar(abs(v.oil_propane_mean_delivery_bat))` per year, respectively.

I propose the creation of a new residential electric rate schedule for heat pump customers. This schedule would set a target revenue that reflects these customers' true cost-of-service. Moving from cost allocation to rate design, I propose a simple flat rate that would recover heat pump customers' allocated cost responsibility. This rate would collect both distribution-related delivery revenue, as well as all other non-distribution delivery revenue, such as transmission and renewable energy surcharges.

By aligning delivery rates with cost causality, this proposal would eliminate the significant cross-subsidy that exists between heat pump and fossil-heating electric residential customers, and it would make it cheaper to heat with heat pumps than natural gas for most Rhode Island households.

<br><br>

**Q.** Please summarize your recommendations to the Commission.
<br>
**A.** I recommend that the Commission:

1. **Create a new residential rate schedule** for heat pump customers, separate from the existing residential rate schedules (A-16 and A-60).
2. **Set the new rate schedule's target revenue equal to heat pump customers' cost-of-service**, as determined by cost-of-service studies that accurately measure the hourly delivery costs that heat pump customers impose on the system.
3. **Introduce flat volumetric delivery rates**, specifically for heat pump customers in the schedule, approximately **`{python} pct(floor(c.hp_flat_volumetric_delivery_pct_below_default * 10) / 10)`** lower than the default volumetric delivery rates, that recover the rate schedule's cost-based target revenue.
4. **Auto-enroll all known _existing_ heat pump customers** into this new rate. Since all customers would save from the switch, no opt-in barrier is needed.
5. **Auto-enroll all _new_ heat pump customers** going through the Clean Heat Rhode Island and the Rhode Island Clean Energy Accelerator programs into this new rate, at the point of installation.
6. **Recalibrate the rate** in each subsequent rate case to reflect evolving cost-of-service as heat pump adoption grows and grid conditions change, providing a transparent and gradual glide path.

<br><br>

**Q.** Are you sponsoring any exhibits with your testimony?
<br>
**A.** Yes. I am sponsoring the following exhibits with my testimony:

- **CLF-JPV-1:** Switchbox memo proposing and analyzing heat pump rate for RIE
- **CLF-JPV-2:** Technical appendix to Switchbox memo describing the methodology and data used
- **CLF-JPV-3:** List of workbooks used to produce the analysis
- **CLF-JPV-4:** Correspondence with RIE's Ryan Constable on winter peaks and forecasts
- **CLF-JPV-5:** RIE Test Year Rates and Revenue


## II. The Cross-Subsidy Problem
**Q.** How much revenue did RIE collect for delivery from residential customersduring the test year, which lasted from September 1st, 2024 to August 31st, 2025?
<br>
**A.** RIE collected `{python} dollar(c.rie_rev_req_total_delivery_rr)` in residential delivery revenue from `{python} comma(int(round(c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count)))` residential customers during the test year. Here is that revenue, broken down by program area:

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/cost_of_service_by_subclass.qmd#fig-rie-rev-req-budgets >}}
:::

RIE collected its residential distribution revenue requirement of `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_customer_charge_total + c.rie_rev_req_core_delivery_total)` million, of which `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_customer_charge_total)` million was raised through customer charges (at $6.00/month), and `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_core_delivery_total)` million was raised through volumetric distribution charges (from `{python} comma(c.rie_rev_req_test_year_residential_kwh)` kWh at `{python} dollar(c.rie_rev_req_core_delivery_usd_per_kwh, 4)`/kWh). These are the rates that RIE is asking to increase in this rate case.

RIE also collected `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_transmission_total)` million raised from transmission charges (`{python} dollar(c.rie_rev_req_transmission_usd_per_kwh, 4)`/kWh), `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_renewable_energy_total)` million for renewable energy programs, `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_energy_efficiency_total)` million for energy efficiency programs, and `{python} dollar_m(c.rie_rev_req_other_delivery_total)` million for other delivery programs.

This figure aggregates the rates and revenues of every bill line item into six high-level program areas; to see the rates and revenues for every line item, consult Exhibit CLF-JPV-5.

<br><br>
**Q.** How much of that revenue was collected from each residential customer subclass?
<br>
**A.** Here is a breakdown of how much revenue RIE collected from customers within each residential subclass:

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(testimony_subclass_delivery_table_html(c))
```
:::

I estimate that heat pump customers make up `{python} hp_pct_of_residential_customers(c)` of RIE's residential customer base. During the test year, however, these customers accounted for `{python} hp_pct_of_residential_delivery_revenue(c)` of RIE's residential delivery revenue.

Goal 6 of the future electric system, adopted by the Commission in Docket 4600, directs that rates should "appropriately charge customers for the cost they impose on the grid."

Under this cost-causation principle, for default rates to be fair, if heat pump customers are contributing `{python} hp_pct_of_residential_delivery_revenue(c)` of RIE's residential delivery revenue, then they must be imposing approximately `{python} hp_pct_of_residential_delivery_revenue(c)` of delivery costs. They are not.

<br><br>
**Q.** What is each residential customer subclass's cost-of-service?
<br>
**A.** Here is a breakdown of each residential customer subclass's cost-of-service, juxtaposed with the revenue RIE collected from them during the test year:

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(Path("cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass.html").read_text())
```
:::

As a group, heat pump customers' cost-of-service was `{python} million(c.cos_default_hp_group_cos)` during the test year, but they paid `{python} million(c.cos_default_hp_group_rev)` through their bills. In other words, while they only made up `{python} hp_pct_of_total_delivery_cos(c)` of total costs, they accounted for `{python} hp_pct_of_total_delivery_revenue(c)` of total revenue.

Heat pump customers therefore paid  `{python} million(c.cos_default_hp_group_xs)` more than their cost of service.

This **overpayment** caused heat pump customers as a group to cover a larger share of RIE's total fixed revenue target of `{python} million(c.rie_rev_req_total_delivery_rr)`, which in turn allowed other groups to pay less than the costs they imposed on the system.

Electric resistance customers also paid `{python} million(c.cos_default_er_overpay_vs_cos)` more than their cost-of-service.

**Q.** What effect do heat pump and electric resistance customers paying more than their cost of service have on the other residential customer subclasses?
<br>
**A.**
Jointly, the overpayments from heat pump and electric resistance customers create a **cross-subsidy** that allows customers who heat with fossil fuels to **underpay** relative to their cost-of-service.

During the test year, for instance, customers who heat with natural gas, which cost the system `{python} million(c.cos_default_ng_group_cos)`, paid only `{python} million(c.cos_default_ng_group_rev)`, or `{python} ng_delivery_rev_as_pct_of_subclass_cos(c)` of their cost-of-service.

Those that heat with delivered fuels, which cost the system `{python} million(c.cos_default_df_group_cos)`, paid only `{python} million(c.cos_default_df_group_rev)`, or `{python} df_delivery_rev_as_pct_of_subclass_cos(c)` of their cost-of-service.

**Q.** How did you measure the cost-of-service for each residential customer subclass?
<br>
**A.** The full methodology is described in Section VII, but I will preview it here.

In brief, I applied the same general cost allocation philosophy that RIE uses in its Allocated Cost-of-Service Study — measure the present-day cost-causation of each customer class, and then use it to divide up the revenue requirement — but I applied it one level deeper, _within_ the residential class, to allocate costs among customer subclasses. I also used a more precise method to measure cost-causation.

My goal was to divide the delivery revenue that RIE collected during the test year—both the distribution revenue requirement, and other delivery costs—into subclass cost-of-service allocations, based on each subclass's cost-causation.

I measured which hours of the test year had the highest marginal delivery costs — for transmission and distribution separately — and then examined how much load each subclass had during those specific hours, using a statistically representative sample of residential load profiles for each subclass.[^nrel_resstock]

[^nrel_resstock]: Using NREL's ResStock end-use load profiles, which are statistically representative energy simulations of Rhode Island's residential building stock.

For each household in the sample, I multiplied its load in each hour by that hour's delivery cost, and summed across all 8,760 hours of the test year to get the total delivery costs that household caused. Summing the household-level costs across all households in a subclass produced the subclass's total delivery costs during the test year.

Once I had each subclass's share of total residential cost-causation, I used those shares to divide up the residential delivery revenue that RIE collected during the test year to arrive at each subclass's cost-of-service. The gap between a subclass's cost-of-service and the revenue RIE actually collects from it under current rates is the cross-subsidy.

By capturing _when_ delivery costs are caused, this hourly allocator measures cost-causation much more accurately than RIE's NCP allocator does, which essentially just measures each class's annual peak demand level—regardless of when it occurs.

An hourly cost-of-service analysis is essential, because if a subclass makes up a small share of the residential customer base, and has its annual peak in winter when the grid may have spare capacity—as heat pump customers do—then the subclass may be causing far fewer costs than its annual peak demand would suggest. (More on this in Section III.)


<br><br>

**Q.** What is the central finding of your cost-of-service study?
<br>
**A.** Under current default residential electric rates (A-16 and A-60), heat pump customers in Rhode Island are paying `{python} f"{c.cos_default_hp_pct_over_cos:.0f}%"` more than their delivery cost-of-service.

<br><br>

**Q.** Why do heat pump customers pay more than their cost-of-service?
<br>
**A.** Because delivery costs are recovered primarily through volumetric per-kWh charges. Volumetric rates assume, by design, that a customer's cost-causation scales with their total electricity consumption: the more kWh you use, the more you pay for delivery. But delivery costs are not caused by every kWh equally. They are driven by load during the hours when the grid is congested and additional demand would trigger the need for upgrades. A kWh consumed during a peak summer hour when a feeder is near capacity contributes to the need for new investment; a kWh consumed during an off-peak winter hour with ample spare capacity does not.

Consider the implication: if a customer consumed a large amount of electricity, but all of it occurred during hours when the grid had spare capacity, that customer would cause essentially no new delivery costs. Yet under volumetric rates, they would pay delivery charges in full proportion to their consumption.

Heat pump customers are close to this scenario. When a household installs a heat pump, its annual electricity consumption grows by roughly `{python} f"{v.elec_pct_change_full_cooling_p25:.0f}"`% to `{python} f"{v.elec_pct_change_full_cooling_p75:.0f}"`% — but the vast majority of that growth is in winter, when Rhode Island's distribution grid has massive spare capacity. As I will show in Section III, not a single one of RIE's feeders is winter-constrained, and the median feeder has `{python} f"{f.median_headroom_winter:.0f}"`% of winter headroom. The new winter load is absorbed by existing infrastructure without triggering upgrades.

Yet under volumetric delivery rates, the customer's delivery bill grows in lockstep with consumption. For instance, if a customer's annual energy usage doubles after installing a heat pump, their delivery bill roughly doubles, but their cost-of-service would grow either modestly (if they are gaining cooling) or would stay the same (if they already have full cooling). The gap is the cross-subsidy.

**Q.** Has this problem been identified before?
<br>
**A.** Yes. In Docket 4770, Walmart's expert witness Gregory Tillman identified the same structural problem for commercial and industrial customers on Rate G-32: when demand-related distribution costs are recovered through volumetric energy charges, high-usage customers systematically overpay and low-usage customers underpay, creating an intra-class subsidy invisible to the standard inter-class cost-of-service analysis. Mr. Tillman estimated a cross-subsidy of `$333` per year per large C&I customer.[^tillman] The dynamic I identify for residential heat pump customers is the same mechanism, operating on the residential side of the meter.

[^tillman]: Direct Testimony of Gregory L. Tillman on behalf of Walmart, Inc., Docket 4770, at TK.

<br><br>


**Q.** Can you illustrate this with a specific example?
<br>
**A.** Yes. Consider a representative single-family home in Cranston — a three-bedroom house built in the 1980s, heated by a natural gas furnace with central air conditioning, with total annual energy bills near the statewide median. This is one of the simulated homes from our ResStock sample, and I use it as a running example in my accompanying report, Exhibit CLF-JPV-1.

When heated by natural gas, this home has an annual electric delivery cost-of-service of `{python} dollar(v.cs_gas_cost)`. Its actual delivery bill is `{python} dollar(v.cs_gas_underpay)` *lower* than that — meaning it underpays for delivery. This is typical for gas-heated homes, as described above.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-cross-subsidy-before-example >}}
:::

Now suppose this home installs a heat pump. Its annual electricity consumption grows by `{python} f"{(v.elec_kwh_multiplier - 1) * 100:.0f}"`%. Because the home already has central air conditioning, all of that growth is in the shoulder seasons and winter — none of it during the summer peak hours that drive grid costs.

::: {.column-body-outset}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-consumption-before-after-state >}}
:::

Under volumetric delivery rates, the home's delivery bill jumps to `{python} dollar(v.cs_hp_bill)` per year. But its delivery cost-of-service barely changes, because none of the new load is during hours when the grid is constrained.

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-cross-subsidy-example >}}
:::

The result is an overpayment of **`{python} dollar(v.cs_hp_bat)` per year**. This overpayment is entirely a consequence of the volumetric rate structure, not of any new costs this home is imposing on the grid.

<br><br>

**Q.** What is the practical consequence of this delivery overpayment for households considering heat pumps?
<br>
**A.** The delivery overpayment I have described is not an abstract accounting problem — it directly determines whether most households in Rhode Island save or lose money when switching to heat pumps.

When a household switches from a gas furnace to a heat pump, its gas consumption drops, while its electric consumption increases. Take the Cranston home as an example:

::: {.column-body-outset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-consumption-elec-focus-state >}}
:::

The home's energy bills follow its energy use: annual gas bills drop, annual electric bills rise—both the supply side of the electric bill, and the delivery side:

::: {.column-body}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-bill-components-decomposed-state >}}
:::

After getting a heat pump, the Cranston home consumes roughly twice as much electricity, so it makes sense that its annual supply bill doubles.

However, as I described in the preceding question, none of the Cranston home's new electricity consumption causes new delivery costs — yet under volumetric delivery rates, its delivery bill nearly doubles as well.

The result is a delivery overpayment of **`{python} dollar(v.cs_hp_bat)` per year**. (See previous question.)

In this case, the overpayment is entirely a consequence of the volumetric rate structure, not of any new costs this home is imposing on the grid. And it is directly responsible for this home losing money after switching to heat pumps: its total energy bills increase by `{python} dollar(v.median_net_increase)` per year.

If, on the other hand, the home's delivery bill reflected its cost-of-service, the Cranston home would actually save `{python} dollar(v.median_energy_total - v.fair_rate_total_bill)` annually after switching to heat pumps:

::::: {.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-bill-decomposed-fair-rate-state >}}
:::::

While this is just one particular home, the example captures a general phenomenon that affects most heat pump customers. Under current default rates, **`{python} pct(1 - v.pct_natgas_save_default_lmi40)`** of gas-heated households in Rhode Island would lose money after switching to heat pumps.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-quadrant-bar-natgas-state-state-lmi-current >}}
:::

The cross-subsidy is directly responsible for why most gas-heated currently lose after switching. As I will demonstrate in Section V, eliminating the cross-subsidy by moving heat pump customers to a separate rate schedule reverses this picture: **`{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_save_hprate_lmi40)`** of gas-heated households would save money after switching to heat pumps.

<br><br>

**Q.** Does this problem extend beyond natural gas-heated households?
<br>
**A.** Yes. The delivery cross-subsidy affects any household that switches to a heat pump, regardless of their current heating fuel. The mechanism is the same: a large increase in electricity consumption — most of it in winter — triggers a proportional increase in delivery charges that does not reflect the actual cost of service for these customers.

To put this in context, RIE's residential customer base breaks down roughly as follows: natural gas-heated households make up `{python} subclass_pct_of_residential_customers(c, "Natural gas")` of residential customers, delivered fuel households (oil and propane) make up `{python} subclass_pct_of_residential_customers(c, "Delivered fuels")`, electric resistance households make up `{python} subclass_pct_of_residential_customers(c, "Electric resistance")`, and heat pump households make up `{python} hp_pct_of_residential_customers(c)`.

**Delivered fuel households**: because oil and propane are more expensive than natural gas, the economics of switching are more favorable — `{python} pct(v.pct_oil_propane_save_default)` of these households would save under current default rates, with average annual savings of `{python} dollar(-v.oil_propane_mean_delta_default)`. But those savings should be more than double: the delivery overpayment reduces the average oil/propane household's annual savings by an average of `{python} dollar(v.oil_propane_delivery_erosion)` per year, turning what should be a compelling economic case (`{python} dollar(-v.oil_propane_mean_delta_hprate / 6)` savings per heating month, on average) into a thinner one (`{python} dollar(-v.oil_propane_mean_delta_default / 6)` per heating month) that fewer households will act on.

**Electric resistance households** already heat with electricity, and because heat pumps are two to three times more efficient than resistance heaters, switching will always reduce their heating bills. `{python} pct(v.pct_elec_resistance_save_default)` would save under current rates, with average annual savings of `{python} dollar(-v.elec_resistance_mean_delta_default)`. But the delivery overpayment still erodes those savings by `{python} dollar(v.elec_resistance_delivery_erosion)` per year — unnecessary charges that do not reflect any costs these households, which are disproportionately lower income,[^er_income] impose on the grid.

[^er_income]: In our ResStock dataset, whose household income data is derived from the Census Bureau's 2019 5-year American Community Survey, the median income of electric resistance households is approximately $28,000, compared to $54,000 for natural gas households and $55,000 for RI's residential population overall.

As I will show in Section V, eliminating the delivery cross-subsidy by moving heat pump customers to a separate rate schedule (with a cost-based revenue target) substantially improves the economics for all three groups.




## III. When Delivery Costs Happen — and When They Don't

**Q.** In Section II, you showed that heat pump customers overpay for delivery relative to their cost-of-service. To understand why, can you first explain what drives delivery costs on RIE's grid?
<br>
**A.** The residential delivery bill contains many line items — customer charges, energy efficiency charges, storm restoration charges, and so on — but the largest components are the demand-related costs of transmission and distribution infrastructure: the poles, wires, substations, and transmission lines that carry electricity to homes.

Those demand-related costs are driven by capacity constraints. When load on a feeder, substation, or transmission line approaches its rated capacity — or reaches a level that would overload remaining equipment under a contingency — the utility must invest in upgrades. It is those upgrades that make up the bulk of what delivery rates recover.

But equipment doesn't approach its limits at all hours equally — these constraints are concentrated in specific times of year, which means the *timing* of a customer's electricity use determines how much infrastructure cost they cause.

<br><br>

**Q.** When do those capacity constraints occur on RIE's system?
<br>
**A.** Exclusively in summer. RIE's own distribution planning engineers have confirmed that "there are no feeders or transformers constrained in the winter."[^clf_jpv_4_8] Using Rhode Island Energy's distribution planning data — feeder and transformer thermal screening, capacity ratings, and its 2024 Electric Peak Forecast — I examined every feeder and substation transformer on the system independently and reached the same conclusion.

[^clf_jpv_4_8]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Response 8.

The chart below ranks each feeder on RIE's distribution grid by how close its forecasted 2026 summer peak comes to its rated normal capacity:

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/feeder_peaks.qmd#fig-feeder-rank-curve-summer >}}
:::

Dark green feeders are those whose summer peak are expected to exceed their summer normal rating; light green feeders are those that are below their normal rating but would overload neighboring equipment under a contingency. Every constrained feeder is summer-constrained.

The key findings from this assessment, as of `{python} f.feeder_rank_year`:

  - Only `{python} pct(f.pct_feeders_any_violation_summer)` of RIE's feeders are thermally constrained — whether under normal loading or under contingency conditions — and every constraint occurs in summer, not winter.
  - **Zero feeders** are winter-constrained at present.
  - Of RIE's `{python} f"{f.cust_total_2026:,.0f}"` customers on active feeders, `{python} pct(f.pct_cust_constrained_2026)` (`{python} f"{f.cust_constrained_2026:,.0f}"` customers) sit on these summer-constrained feeders.
  - For most of these feeders, the utility is already planning capacity upgrades or system reconfigurations that will reduce their summer peak loads.
  - As for substation transformers, only `{python} pct(f.pct_xfmr_any_violation_summer)` are constrained — again, all in summer.


<br><br>

**Q.** How did you incorporate these engineering findings into your cost-of-service analysis?
<br>
**A.** The feeder and transformer analysis establishes that all of RIE's capacity constraints are summer-driven. But RIE's planning data only reports each feeder's summer peak load level, not the hour at which that peak occurs or the feeder's hourly load profile for the year. RIE has confirmed that the hour of peak "is not tracked as it is not necessary to current distribution planning practices focused on peak level analysis."[^clf_jpv_4_4] Therefore, the data does not tell us *which specific summer hours* tend to drive the constraints.

[^clf_jpv_4_4]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Response 4.

To allocate delivery costs to individual customers based on when their load falls, I needed to identify the highest-stress hours on Rhode Island's bulk transmission and RIE's distribution grid, and then spread marginal delivery cost across only those hours — reflecting the fact that load during hours near the system peak is what drives the need for new upgrades, while load during all other hours imposes no incremental capacity costs.

For reasons detailed in the full methodology, Section VII, I used the top 100 hours of aggregate New England system load for bulk transmission and the top 100 hours of RIE's system load for distribution and sub-transmission.

The heatmaps below show the resulting hourly cost profiles for every hour of the test year. Each tile is one hour; darker tiles indicate higher marginal cost.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/delivery_cost_timing.qmd#fig-heatmap-bulk-tx >}}
:::

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/delivery_cost_timing.qmd#fig-heatmap-dist-sub-tx >}}
:::

The summer concentration that results is stark: delivery capacity costs are confined to a narrow band of summer afternoon and evening hours. Every winter hour is zero.

<br><br>

**Q.** How much spare capacity does the distribution grid have in winter?
<br>
**A.** The feeder analysis from RIE's own engineering data answers this directly. As before, the chart below plots every feeder's peak load as a percentage of its rated summer normal capacity, for both summer (orange) and winter (blue), ranked from most to least stressed.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/feeder_peaks.qmd#fig-feeder-rank-curve-summer-and-winter >}}
:::

Feeder winter peaks sit far below their summer peaks throughout RIE's distribution grid: no feeder is winter constrained, **`{python} f"{ceil(f.pct_feeders_headroom_gt_30_winter * 100):.0f}"`%** of feeders have more than 30% winter headroom, and the **median winter headroom is `{python} f"{f.median_headroom_winter:.0f}"`%**.

It is worth noting that the winter peaks shown here are estimates. RIE's distribution planning group does not compile historical winter peaks for its feeders or substation transformers. When we requested this data through discovery, RIE explained that "the RI system is currently summer limited" and that "the Company concentrates on summer analysis for efficiency reasons," adding: "If the system is summer limited and it is planned sufficiently, it will be sufficient in the winter."[^clf_jpv_4_6]

RIE does not forecast feeder-level winter peaks either, stating that "a winter analysis would provide no useful information to determine system sufficiency."[^clf_jpv_4_12]

[^clf_jpv_4_6]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Response 6.

[^clf_jpv_4_12]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Response 12.

RIE's engineers recommended that we estimate each feeder's winter peak as 70% of its measured 2024 summer peak, consistent with the current ratio of system-wide winter to summer peak demand. This is the same ratio RIE's distribution planning team uses internally — for instance, when the Control Center requests loading guidance during a winter contingency event.[^clf_jpv_4_11] I followed that recommendation.

[^clf_jpv_4_11]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Responses 10–11.

<br><br>

**Q.** What does this winter headroom mean for heat pump customers' delivery costs?
<br>
**A.** It means that the vast majority of the new electricity a heat pump adds to the grid in winter causes no new delivery costs whatsoever.

When a household installs a heat pump, its electricity consumption grows substantially — but almost all of that growth is in winter, when RIE's distribution grid has massive spare capacity. That load is absorbed by existing infrastructure without triggering upgrades, and therefore without causing delivery costs.

<br><br>

**Q.** Does this mean that heat pump customers have the same cost-of-service as other customers?
<br>
**A.** No — heat pump customers actually have a somewhat higher average delivery cost-of-service than other customers subclasses:


:::{.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(Path("cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass_avg.html").read_text())
```
:::

The average delivery cost-of-service for heat pump customers is `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_cost)` per year, compared to `{python} dollar(v.natgas_mean_delivery_cost)` for natural gas, `{python} dollar(v.oil_propane_mean_delivery_cost)` for delivered fuel, and `{python} dollar(v.er_mean_delivery_cost)` for electric resistance.

As I'll show, the main variable that explains this variation in cost-of-service between residential customer subclasses is the prevalence of summer cooling among their members — not winter heating load.

(In spite of heat pump customers' cooling-driven higher cost-of-service, the cross-subsidy still arises, because volumetric rates effectively charge for *all* of their winter load as if it were causing delivery costs, when it is not.)

In our modeling, every home that installs a heat pump gains full cooling capability. In ducted dwellings, if the heat pump replaces an existing central air conditioner, it usually provides more efficient cooling. Dwellings without ducts typically have window units cooling some rooms but not others, or no cooling at all. Installing ductless mini-splits throughout the home to provide primary heating also means providing cooling to every conditioned room — a substantial increase in cooling coverage.

Either way, we model `{python} pct(v.hp_share_of_households)` of Rhode Island households that currently have heat pumps are modeled as having full cooling capacity, and we assume they use it to maintain a comfortable setpoint all summer long.[^cooling_conservative]

That is not the case for other customer subclasses: in Rhode Island, only `{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_full_cooling)` of natural gas-heated households, `{python} pct(v.pct_er_full_cooling)` of electric resistance-heated households, and `{python} pct(v.pct_oil_propane_full_cooling)` of delivered fuel-heated households have full cooling.[^cooling_source]

[^cooling_conservative]: This is a conservative assumption that overstates heat pump customers' cost-of-service, since some of these households are likely cost-sensitive and would curtail their cooling during hot summer days — precisely the hours that drive delivery costs.

[^cooling_source]: Based on cooling equipment distributions from EIA's 2020 Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS).

This matters because cooling is what generates load during the high-cost summer hours that drive grid investment.

<br><br>

**Q.** Can you illustrate how a heat pump customer's summer cooling affects its delivery cost-of-service?
<br>
**A.** Consider a home that had zero air conditioning before installing a heat pump:

:::{.column-body-outset}
{{< embed notebooks/delivery_cost_timing.qmd#fig-consumption-cooling-gain >}}
:::

Their new cooling load causes their electrical use in July and August to increase by roughly `{python} f"{d.cooling_gain_jul_aug_pct_change * 100:.0f}"`%.

Some of this incremental demand occurs during the summer hours that drive delivery costs—the orange sliver represents the kWh the household consumed during this high-cost hours.

This is the portion of new load that actually causes delivery costs, and it is why this home's cost-of-service increases.

However, only `{python} f"{d.cooling_gain_jja_pct_of_new_load * 100:.0f}"`% of this home's new electricity consumption occurs between June and August, and only `{python} f"{d.cooling_gain_pct_new_cost_triggering * 100:.1f}"`% occurs during the specific hours that drive delivery costs.

Despite the magnitude of their new winter load, it causes no new delivery costs, because Rhode Island's transmission and distribution grids have winter headroom at present.

<br><br>

**Q.** What about homes that already had full cooling before switching to a heat pump?
<br>
**A.** Their delivery cost-of-service often *decreases*. Return to the Cranston home from Section II, which had central air conditioning before installing its heat pump:

:::{.column-body-outset}
{{< embed notebooks/delivery_cost_timing.qmd#fig-consumption-cranston-enhanced >}}
:::

After the switch, its summer demand in July and August decreased by `{python} f"{abs(d.cranston_jul_aug_pct_change) * 100:.0f}"`% — a load reduction visible as the faded segments in the summer months.

More efficient cooling during the hottest hours of summer caused their usage during high-cost hours to decrease, as well, by `{python} f"{abs(d.cranston_cost_hour_pct_change) * 100:.0f}"`%.

Consequently, the home's delivery cost-of-service drops from `{python} dollar(v.cs_gas_cost)` to `{python} dollar(v.cs_hp_cost)`.

In other words, this home would become marginally *cheaper* to serve, even as its delivery bill jumps from `{python} dollar(v.cs_gas_bill)` to `{python} dollar(v.cs_hp_bill)`.

In other words, the cost-of-service difference between heat pump customers and other customers is real, but modest — driven entirely by differences in summer cooling prevalence, not by the winter heating load that makes up the bulk of their electricity consumption.

<br><br>

**Q.** If heat pump customers' cost-of-service is only somewhat higher than other customers', why are their delivery bills so much higher?
<br>
**A.** Because volumetric delivery rates treat every kilowatt-hour as if it causes costs equally. A kWh consumed during a winter hour with `{python} f"{f.median_headroom_winter:.0f}"`% spare feeder capacity is charged the same delivery rate as a kWh consumed during a summer afternoon when a feeder is at the edge of its thermal limit. But only the latter drives the need for grid upgrades.

Heat pump customers' annual electricity consumption grows by roughly `{python} f"{v.elec_pct_change_full_cooling_p25:.0f}"`% to `{python} f"{v.elec_pct_change_full_cooling_p75:.0f}"`%, and their delivery bills grow proportionally. But almost all of that growth is in winter — absorbed for free by existing infrastructure. Their cost-of-service is `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_cost / v.ff_mean_delivery_cost - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher than that of fossil-fuel-heated customers, but their delivery bills are `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_bill / v.ff_mean_delivery_bill - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher. That gap — the difference between a modest cost-of-service increase and a much larger bill increase — is the cross-subsidy.

<br><br>

**Q.** Will this dynamic change as heat pump adoption grows?
<br>
**A.** Eventually, but not for at least 15 years, according to current forecasts.

RIE's most recent heat pump adoption forecast projects that between `{python} pct(f.pct_hp_base_2039)` and `{python} pct(f.pct_hp_high_2039)` of its electric customers will have heat pumps by 2039:

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/feeder_peaks.qmd#fig-hp-adoption >}}
:::

What impact will this pace of adoption have on RIE's distribution grid?

The chart below shows RIE's forecasted summer and winter net peak demand through 2039, with and without heat pump adoption.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/feeder_peaks.qmd#fig-system-peak-forecast >}}
:::

While heat pump adoption would cause the system-wide winter peak to rise significantly, it would reach only about **`{python} pct(f.winter_pct_of_summer_2039)`** of the summer peak by 2039. (RIE actually assumes that heat pumps would _reduce_ summer peaks as they become more widespread due to more efficient cooling.)

What about the feeder level?

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/feeder_peaks.qmd#fig-feeder-winter-shift >}}
:::

**Zero feeders** are projected to become winter-constrained due to winter peak growth, and **`{python} pct(f.pct_feeders_headroom_gt_20_winter_2039)`** of feeders would still have more than 20% winter headroom in 2039.

RIE's own distribution planning engineers corroborate this timeline, estimating in correspondence that "the system and underlying components will not become winter-limited until the mid to late 2030s".[^clf_jpv_4_14]

[^clf_jpv_4_14]: Exhibit CLF-JPV-4, Response 14.

It's important to note that RIE provided the feeder-level summer peak forecasts in the chart above, but not the winter peak forecasts. At present, the company does not compile historical winter peaks for individual feeders, and it does not forecast these peaks into the future.

To produce such forecasts for 2039, I followed RIE's recommended method: I estimate each feeder's current winter peak as 70% of its measured summer peak. I then projected each feeder's winter peak forward using the system-wide winter peak growth rates from RIE's 2024 Electric Peak Forecast. This is the best available method to forecast feeder-level winter peaks, given RIE's data limitations.



## IV. Why the Commission Should Act: Docket 4600 Principles and Goals

**Q.** Why should the Commission address this cross-subsidy in the current proceeding?
<br>
**A.** Because the cross-subsidy directly contradicts the rate design framework the Commission adopted in Docket 4600.

In Docket 4600, the Commission established 12 rate design principles and 8 goals for the future electric system. The most directly relevant is Goal 6 — "Appropriately charge customers for the cost they impose on the grid."

As I have shown, heat pump customers' delivery cost-of-service is only `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_cost / v.ff_mean_delivery_cost - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher than that of fossil-fuel-heated customers, but their delivery bills are `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_bill / v.ff_mean_delivery_bill - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher. That gap is not an appropriate charge for the cost they impose. It is an overcharge of `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per customer per year.

The Docket 4600 framework was designed to guide exactly this kind of decision. Leaving the cross-subsidy in place while the Commission has the evidence to correct it would be inconsistent with the principles the Commission adopted to govern rate design in Rhode Island.

<br><br>

**Q.** Order 22851 directs that any party proposing a rate design must address how the proposal advances, detracts from, or is neutral as to each of the rate design principles and goals adopted in Docket 4600. How does the heat pump rate perform against the 12 rate design principles?
<br>
**A.** The proposal advances 10 of the 12 principles, detracts from none, and is neutral on 2. I will address each in turn.

1. **Safe, reliable, affordable, environmentally responsible service.** Advances. The proposal improves affordability for heat pump customers by eliminating the `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` annual delivery overpayment. It advances environmental responsibility by removing the operating cost barrier that discourages electrification of heating. Safety and reliability are unaffected — this is a billing change, not a grid change.

2. **Economic efficiency.** Advances. Under current rates, heat pump customers' delivery bills are `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_bill / v.ff_mean_delivery_bill - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher than those of fossil-fuel-heated customers — but their actual delivery cost-of-service is only `{python} f"{(v.hp_mean_delivery_cost / v.ff_mean_delivery_cost - 1) * 100:.0f}"`% higher. The current rate overstates the cost of electric heating and understates the cost of fossil-fuel heating, distorting the investment decisions of every homeowner weighing whether to switch. Correcting this improves economic efficiency.

3. **Price signals reflecting long-run marginal cost.** Advances. Volumetric delivery rates currently signal that winter electricity consumption is expensive to deliver. As I have shown, marginal distribution capacity costs are effectively zero in winter. The heat pump rate corrects this by aligning delivery charges with the actual temporal pattern of delivery costs.

4. **Address externalities.** Advances. The cross-subsidy functions as a financial penalty on electrification — an externality imposed by rate design, not by physics. Removing it allows the operating cost advantage of heat pumps to be realized, enabling households to reduce their combustion of fossil fuels.

5. **Empower consumers to manage costs.** Advances. Under current rates, a household considering a heat pump sees an inflated estimate of what electric heating would cost, because volumetric delivery rates charge for winter load as though it were causing grid costs. Accurate pricing enables informed decisions about heating investments.

6. **Utility cost recovery and revenue stability.** Neutral. Total residential class revenue is unchanged — the proposal reallocates revenue responsibility within the class, it does not reduce it. Revenue stability is maintained through the existing decoupling mechanism.

7. **Fair compensation for value and services.** Advances. This principle is at the heart of the proposal. Heat pump customers currently pay `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per year more than the delivery services they receive. The heat pump rate corrects this by setting delivery charges in proportion to delivery cost-of-service.

8. **Transparent and understandable.** Neutral. The heat pump rate is a flat volumetric rate — simple to understand and straightforward to implement. Creating a new subclass adds structural complexity to the tariff, but the rationale is intuitive: customers should pay for the costs they cause, not more.

9. **Gradualism.** Advances. Acting now, while heat pump penetration is approximately `{python} pct(v.hp_share_of_households)` of residential customers, *is* the gradual approach. The average impact on non-heat pump customers would be approximately `{python} dollar(v.rebal_nonhp_mean_monthly_delta, 2)` per month — well within the range the Commission has historically considered reasonable. Delay would not avoid the correction; it would make the eventual correction larger and less gradual, as the number of overpaying customers grows. The heat pump rate itself would be recalibrated in future rate cases as cost-of-service evolves, ensuring continued gradualism.

10. **Energy burden and low-income needs.** Advances. Low-income households that install heat pumps face both the delivery overpayment and high energy burdens. Eliminating the overpayment directly reduces their energy costs. Combined with the LMI discount I propose in Section VI, the heat pump rate would meaningfully improve the economics of electrification for the households that need affordability most.

11. **Consistent with policy goals.** Advances. Rhode Island's Act on Climate and Resilient Rhode Island Act set binding targets for greenhouse gas emissions reductions that require widespread electrification of heating. Under current default rates, only `{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_save_default_lmi40)` of low-income gas-heated households would save money by switching to heat pumps. Under the heat pump rate, `{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_save_hprate_lmi40)` would save. The delivery overpayment is one of the largest obstacles to the electrification these laws require.

12. **Encourage investments enabling the future energy system.** Advances. The Docket 4600 Stakeholder Report — produced by a working group that included Narragansett Electric Company — observed that "lower volumetric charges (kWh) appear to provide higher incentives for electrification strategies" (Section 3.5). That is precisely what the heat pump rate does: it lowers the volumetric delivery charge for the customers whose winter load does not cause delivery costs, aligning the price signal with the grid reality.

<br><br>

**Q.** How does the proposal perform against the 8 goals of the future electric system?
<br>
**A.** It advances 6 of the 8 goals, detracts from none, and is neutral on 2.

1. **Reliable, safe, clean, affordable energy.** Advances — the proposal improves affordability and removes a barrier to clean energy adoption.

2. **Strengthen the Rhode Island economy and create jobs.** Advances — removing the operating cost barrier to heat pumps supports the growth of the HVAC workforce and the state's clean energy economy.

3. **Address the challenges of climate change.** Advances — the proposal directly addresses the rate-design barrier to building electrification, which is essential to meeting the state's emissions targets.

4. **Facilitate customer investment in electrification of heating and vehicles.** Advances — this goal uses the exact phrase "electrification of heating." A rate that penalizes electric heating is inconsistent with a goal that seeks to facilitate it.

5. **Appropriately compensate DERs for the value they provide.** Neutral — this goal primarily concerns compensation for distributed generation and storage. The heat pump rate does not affect DER compensation.

6. **Appropriately charge customers for the cost they impose on the grid.** Advances — this is the core goal, and the core of this testimony. The heat pump rate aligns delivery charges with delivery cost-of-service.

7. **Appropriately compensate the utility for the services it provides.** Neutral — total revenue is unchanged. The utility collects the same amount; the proposal changes how that amount is allocated among customers.

8. **Align utility, customer, and policy objectives.** Advances — the current rate structure creates a contradiction: the state's policy objectives require electrification of heating, while the rate structure penalizes customers who electrify. The heat pump rate resolves this contradiction.

<br><br>

**Q.** Did the Docket 4600 Working Group anticipate the kind of cost-shifting problem documented in this testimony?
<br>
**A.** It did. The Stakeholder Report explicitly called for investigation into cost-shifting between customer groups:

> "The Commission should investigate potential cost shifting and equity concerns over time as distributed generation and other types of DERs become more widespread." (Section 3.5)

And it posed a direct question that this testimony answers:

> "What is the appropriate way to measure any cost-shift between DER and non-DER customers and at what level does it become unreasonable?" (Section 3.6)

Our analysis provides one answer to that question. We measured the cost-shift using the methodology described in Section VII: it is `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per heat pump customer per year, totaling millions of dollars in aggregate.


## V. Proposed Remedy and Impact

**Q.** What do you recommend the Commission do to address the cross-subsidy?
<br>
**A.** I recommend that the Commission create a new residential delivery rate subclass for heat pump customers, with a target revenue based on their actual delivery cost-of-service. T

his is fundamentally a **cost allocation** correction — the same kind of correction the Commission makes when it finds that one customer class is paying more or less than its cost-of-service, except applied one level down, within the residential class.

<br><br>

**Q.** Why is a new subclass necessary? Could the existing rate be adjusted instead?
<br>
**A.** The cross-subsidy is structural: it arises from the interaction between volumetric delivery rates and the fact that heat pump customers consume substantially more electricity than other residential customers, predominantly in winter. As long as heat pump customers remain on the same rate schedule as everyone else, volumetric rates will overcollect from them — regardless of how the rate level is set.

Broadly speaking, there are two ways to eliminate this kind of intra-class cross-subsidy.

The first is through cost allocation: creating a separate subclass with a revenue requirement that reflects the subgroup's actual cost-of-service.

The second is through rate design: dramatically overhauling the default rate, such as significantly increasing the fixed customer charge for the entire residential class, shifting cost recovery away from volumetric charges. This approach would reduce the overcollection from high-use customers, but it is highly disruptive, regressive — it raises bills for low-use customers, who are disproportionately low-income — and it would weaken incentives for energy efficiency and distributed generation. In Docket 4770, nearly every intervenor opposed National Grid's proposal to increase the residential customer charge from $5 to $8.50 for exactly these reasons.

I recommend the first approach. A new subclass corrects the cost allocation directly and simply, without fundamentally redesigning the rate structure for RIE's `{python} comma(int(round(c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count)))` residential customers. It moves approximately `{python} pct(v.hp_share_of_households)` of residential customers to a new schedule and slightly adjusts volumetric delivery rates for the rest. The cross-subsidy is eliminated with minimal disruption.

<br><br>

**Q.** What rate design do you propose for the heat pump subclass?
<br>
**A.** A flat volumetric delivery rate, lower than the current default, calibrated so that heat pump customers' average annual delivery revenue equals their delivery cost-of-service. The delivery cost-of-service for heat pump customers is `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_cost)` per customer per year, on average. Under the current default rate, the utility collects `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bill)` per customer per year from these same customers. The difference — `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per year — is the overpayment that the new rate would eliminate.

The table below shows the proposed rate alongside the current default:

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(Path("cache/rate_table.html").read_text())
```

<br><br>

**Q.** Why a flat rate rather than a seasonal or time-varying rate?
<br>
**A.** Four reasons.

First, a flat rate makes the cost allocation correction maximally transparent. The rate is simply the heat pump subclass's allocated delivery cost responsibility, minus revenue from fixed charges, divided by the subclass's total kilowatt-hours. The correction is visible and verifiable.

Second, a flat rate is simple. It is a single volumetric charge applied to all heat pump customers in all hours. Customers and stakeholders can understand what it does and why.

Third, a flat rate guarantees that every heat pump customer saves relative to the default. This is important because it means that all known heat pump customers can be auto-enrolled in the new rate without risk of harm to any individual customer. I discuss enrollment further below.

Fourth, a flat rate works with today's metering infrastructure. A time-varying rate (beyond a simple seasonal rate) would require interval metering that most residential customers do not yet have. The cost allocation correction should not wait for AMI deployment.

I want to be clear: the flat rate is a starting point, not a permanent design. Once AMI is deployed, the Commission should consider transitioning heat pump customers to cost-reflective time-varying rates that recover the same correctly sized revenue requirement. A seasonal time-of-use rate would add economic efficiency to the fairness that the flat rate establishes.

<br><br>

**Q.** Is the heat pump rate revenue-neutral?
<br>
**A.** Yes, at the residential class level. The proposal reallocates delivery revenue responsibility within the residential class — it does not reduce total residential revenue.

Heat pump customers' delivery revenue would decrease to match their cost-of-service, and the volumetric delivery rates for non-heat pump customers remaining on the default schedule would be adjusted upward slightly to recover the difference. The total delivery revenue collected from all residential customers would be unchanged.

<br><br>

**Q.** How much would non-heat pump customers' bills increase?
<br>
**A.** By approximately **`{python} dollar(v.rebal_nonhp_mean_monthly_delta, 2)` per month** on average. Heat pump customers make up only `{python} pct(v.hp_share_of_households)` of residential customers, so the total rebalanced amount — approximately `{python} f"${v.hp_total_cross_subsidy_delivery_millions:,.1f}"` million per year — is spread across `{python} comma(int(round(v.nonhp_households_millions * 1_000_000, -3)))` non-heat pump households.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-bill-change-non-hp-rebal-state >}}
:::

The result is a modest adjustment: **`{python} pct(v.rebal_nonhp_pct_decrease + v.rebal_nonhp_pct_increase_under_5)`** of non-heat pump households would see their monthly delivery bill change by less than `{python} dollar(5)`.


<br><br>

**Q.** What would happen to heat pump customers' energy bills under the proposed rate?
<br>
**A.** The heat pump rate would transform the economics of switching to heat pumps in Rhode Island.

Under the current default rate, only **`{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_save_default_lmi40)`** of gas-heated households would save money on their total energy bills after switching to a heat pump.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-quadrant-bar-natgas-switch-hprate-state-lmi-current >}}
:::
 Under the heat pump rate, **`{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_save_hprate_lmi40)`** would save. The share that would lose more than $1,000 per year would drop from `{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_lose_1k_default_lmi40)` to `{python} pct(v.pct_natgas_lose_1k_hprate_lmi40)`.

The improvement is equally striking for other heating types. Oil and propane households would see their average annual savings grow from `{python} dollar(abs(v.oil_propane_mean_delta_default))` under the default rate to **`{python} dollar(abs(v.oil_propane_mean_delta_hprate))`** under the heat pump rate, and the share that saves would rise from `{python} pct(v.pct_oil_propane_save_default)` to **`{python} pct(v.pct_oil_propane_save_hprate)`**.

For electric resistance households — who already heat with electricity but use it inefficiently — average annual savings would grow from `{python} dollar(abs(v.elec_resistance_mean_delta_default))` to **`{python} dollar(abs(v.elec_resistance_mean_delta_hprate))`**, and the share saving would rise from `{python} pct(v.pct_elec_resistance_save_default)` to **`{python} pct(v.pct_elec_resistance_save_hprate)`**.

<br><br>

**Q.** How does the cross-subsidy affect low-and-moderate income households specifically?
<br>
**A.** LMI heat pump customers suffer from the same per-household delivery overpayment as non-LMI heat pump customers — but the overpayment hits them harder, because it represents a larger share of their income.

At present, `{python} pct(v.pct_burdened_current_lmi40)` of LMI households are highly energy burdened, meaning they pay more than 6% of their annual income on energy bill (assuming current LMI discount enrollment levels of approximately `{python} f"{v.lmi_current_enrollment_pct:.0f}"`%.)

If they switched to heat pumps under the default electric rates, the share of highly burdened LMI households would rise to **`{python} pct(v.pct_burdened_hp_default_lmi40)`**.


:::{.column-page-inset-right}
{{< embed notebooks/analysis.qmd#fig-burden-bar-lmi-current >}}
:::

However, if LMI households that install heat pumps had access to the heat pump rate paired and were fully enrolled in the LMI discount program, that share would drop to **`{python} pct(v.pct_burdened_hp_flat_lmi100)`** — well *below* the current high-burden rate of `{python} pct(v.pct_burdened_current_lmi40)`.

In other words, electrification combined with the heat pump rate and LMI discount enrollment would not just avoid worsening energy burdens — it would significantly reduce them.

The implication is clear: LMI electrification should always be paired with both the heat pump rate and LMI discount enrollment. Any program that installs heat pumps in low-income homes without also enrolling those households in the HP rate and the applicable LMI discount is potentially making energy burdens worse. Conversely, pairing electrification with both rate and discount enrollment would make heat pumps a vehicle for *reducing* energy burdens, not increasing them.

<br><br>

**Q.** How should heat pump customers be enrolled in the new rate?
<br>
**A.** Through automatic enrollment, building on the approach Massachusetts has adopted for its heat pump rate.

For existing heat pump customers, the utility should auto-enroll all customers it can identify as having heat pumps. Because every customer saves under the heat pump rate compared to the default, there is no adverse selection concern — every enrolled customer benefits.

For new heat pump customers, enrollment should occur at the point of installation through Clean Heat Rhode Island and the Rhode Island Clean Energy Accelerator. Every customer receiving a heat pump incentive should be automatically placed on the heat pump rate.

<br><br>

**Q.** How would the heat pump rate evolve over time?
<br>
**A.** The rate should be recalibrated in each rate case to reflect the evolving delivery cost-of-service for heat pump customers.

he rate I propose already reflects the fact that heat pump customers contribute to summer distribution capacity costs through their cooling load. As heat pump adoption grows and winter peaks eventually approach summer peaks, heat pump customers' winter load will begin to trigger distribution investments, and their cost-of-service will rise. The rate would rise accordingly — a natural glide path, not a cliff.

However, as I showed in Section III, RIE engineering data and peak forecasts project that nearly all feeders will remain unconstrained in winter until the early 2040s. Until that point in time, the flat heat pump rate would remain substantially below the RIE's default flat rate.

The Commission revisits rates regularly. Each rate case provides an opportunity to update the cost-of-service analysis and recalibrate the rate. This is a far more prudent approach than maintaining an overcharge for years while waiting for grid conditions to change.


## VI. Anticipated Objections

This section addresses alternatives to the HP subclass that the Company or other parties may propose, and explains why each is either insufficient or complementary.

**Q.** Is a technology-specific rate the only way to fix the cross-subsidy?
<br>
**A.** No. As I noted, you could also dramatically increase the fixed customer charge for the entire residential class, shifting cost recovery away from volumetric charges. But this was opposed by nearly every intervenor in Docket 4770 (Rabago, LeBel, Athas, the George Wiley Center) because higher fixed charges are regressive, weaken efficiency incentives, and insulate the utility from demand-side innovation. A technology-specific rate achieves the same cost allocation correction without those harms.

<br><br>

**Q.** How does your proposal this compare to Massachusetts's heat pump rate?
<br>
# TODO: explain v1 vs v2 rates, explain that v2 is more aligned on cost of service. instead of flat they do a kind of funny seasonal rate where they keep summer the same as now and way lower winter. According to our previously analysis this keeps delivery revenue collection roughly the same before and after someone gets HP if they get on the rate. (They didn't want to raise the summer rate, even if it might have been more cost-reflective, because then they wouldn't auto-enroll everyone.) We propose a flat rate, which is simpler and makes the cost allocation correction more transparent. As long as it is based on a separate rate class with a target revenue equal to actual cost of service, either rate design will eliminate the cross-subsidy. The RIPUC could picke either—the rate design choice is secondary to getting the "heat pump revenue requirement" right.

<br><br>

**Q.** Rhode Island Energy has suggested that time-of-use rates, deployed alongside AMI, would address the issues your testimony identifies. Would TOU rates eliminate the cross-subsidy?
<br>
**A.** No. TOU and the HP subclass address fundamentally different problems:

  - **TOU** addresses **within-day peak management**: it shifts load from expensive peak hours to cheaper off-peak hours. It improves efficiency.
  - **The HP subclass** addresses **structural overcollection**: volumetric rates collect more from HP customers than their cost-of-service, regardless of which hours they consume electricity. It improves fairness.
  - A TOU rate that recovers the same class-level revenue requirement will still overcollect from HP customers because the overcollection is driven by volume, not timing.

<br><br>

**Q.** Can you demonstrate this?
<br>
**A.** Yes. We designed a cost-reflective TOU delivery rate for RIE and applied it to HP customer load profiles. Even under TOU, the cross-subsidy persists — because TOU redistributes revenue collection across hours, but does not change the total collected from high-use customers within the class. [TK: embed TOU comparison figure once INCLUDE_TOU runs are available.]

<br><br>

**Q.** Are TOU rates and the HP subclass mutually exclusive?
<br>
**A.** No. They are complementary:

  - Create the HP subclass now to fix the cost allocation (fairness).
  - Layer TOU on top when AMI is deployed to improve price signals (efficiency).
  - Massachusetts is doing exactly this — a seasonal HP rate now, transitioning to include TOU as AMI rolls out.
  - Fairness first, then efficiency. But doing efficiency alone does not deliver fairness.

However, waiting years until AMI deployment is complete and TOU rates are developed and rolled out means accepting years of continued overcollection from heat pump customers. And as we note above, TOU rates alone won't fix the cross-subsidy—cost allocation is the fundamental problem. The cross-subsidy exists today — `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per year per HP customer — and the Commission should not defer correcting it.


## VII. Methodology: How We Measured the Cross-Subsidy

In RIE's ACOSS, the utility measures cost-causation at the class level using a Non-Coincident Peak (NCP) allocator: it looks at each class's annual peak demand, it sums up these class-level demands into a "total NCP demand", and uses each class's _share_ of this total NCP demand to divide the system-wide delivery revenue requirement among classes.

**Q.** What methodology did you use to measure the delivery cost-of-service for heat pump and non-heat pump customers?
<br>
TODO: give the number and cite where we got it from and that engineers told us to use it.
**A.** A marginal cost-of-service (MCOS) analysis, implemented through what we call the Bill Alignment Test. The methodology is described in detail in my attached exhibit [TK]. In summary:

  - We assign hourly marginal costs to each simulated household's hourly load profile over a full year (8,760 hours).
  - The hourly marginal costs include four components: marginal energy costs, marginal generation capacity costs, marginal bulk transmission capacity costs, and marginal distribution capacity costs.
  - The sum of hourly marginal costs for a household is its marginal cost-of-service, or the new costs that it caused in 2025.
  - We then sum the marginal cost-of-service for all households in each subclass. We look at what share of all the new cost each subclass caused, and we then apply this share to the (portion of) the revenue requirement to arrive at their subclass cost allocation. (This is similar to the NCP allocator used by RIE, which is an attempt to measure a class's cost-of-service compared to other classes, and then use that to allocat the revenue requirement.)
  - We sum up all the bills for the households in each subclass, and compare it to the subclass cost allocation. The difference is the cross-subsidy.

TODO: the full "How did you measure this?" detail from the original Section II is preserved here. The brief version in Section II is a forward reference to this section.

<br><br>

**Q.** What marginal cost components did you include, and what are their sources?
<br>
**A.**

  | Component | Source of annualized cost | Hourly allocation |
  |---|---|---|
  | Marginal energy | NREL Cambium (hourly, BA-specific) | Every hour |
  | Generation capacity | NREL Cambium (annual capacity cost) | Top ~100 system load hours |
  | Bulk transmission | AESC 2024 avoided PTF cost ($69/kW-year)[^aesc_cite] | Top 100 aggregate New England system load hours |
  | Distribution capacity | AESC 2024 avoided distribution capacity cost ($80.24/kW-year)[^aesc_cite] | Top 100 RIE system load hours (summer only) |

[^aesc_cite]: Synapse Energy Economics et al., *Avoided Energy Supply Components of New England: 2024 Report* (AESC 2024), February 2024, amended May 2024. Available at https://www.synapse-energy.com/aesc-2024-materials.

<br><br>

**Q.** How did you allocate delivery capacity costs to specific hours?
<br>
**A.** Using a standard peak-responsibility approach from marginal cost-of-service analysis: the annualized cost of new capacity investment is spread across only the hours most likely to drive that investment, and all other hours receive zero cost.

For both bulk transmission and distribution, the annualized capacity costs come from the AESC 2024 study — the standard avoided cost input used by all New England states, including Rhode Island, for energy efficiency cost-effectiveness screening under Docket 4600.[^aesc_cite]

  - For **bulk transmission**, I allocated the AESC's avoided Pool Transmission Facility cost of \$69/kW-year across the top 100 hours of aggregate New England system load — the hours when regional transmission facilities are most stressed.
  - For **distribution and sub-transmission**, I allocated the AESC's avoided distribution capacity cost of \$80.24/kW-year across the top 100 hours of RIE's system load, which serves as a proxy for when local feeders and substations are most stressed.

Ideally, the distribution allocation would use hourly load profiles for each individual feeder, identifying the specific hours when each constrained feeder approaches its limit. RIE does not publish feeder-level 8760 load data,[^rie_correspondence] so the aggregate system load approach is a reasonable first approximation.[^feeder_8760]

[^rie_correspondence]: [TK: cite correspondence with RIE engineers confirming this limitation.]

[^feeder_8760]: If feeder-level hourly data were available, the high-cost hours would likely be spread across more hours — since different feeders peak at somewhat different times — with lower per-hour costs. The aggregate approach concentrates costs in fewer hours, which if anything overstates the cost assigned to any individual peak hour.

<br><br>

**Q.** How did you estimate the annualized marginal cost of distribution capacity?
<br>
**A.** This is a critical input, so I will describe it in detail.

  - We obtained RIE's feeder-level thermal screening data, which contains each feeder's summer peak load and normal/emergency capacity ratings. This is the same data RIE uses for distribution planning.
  - We identified which feeders are constrained (peak exceeds normal capacity or creates N-1 contingency risk). Only `{python} pct(f.pct_feeders_any_violation_summer)` of feeders are constrained, all in summer. Zero feeders are winter-constrained.
  - For the annualized marginal cost of capacity additions, we used the AESC 2024 avoided distribution capacity cost of \$80.24/kW-year.[^aesc_cite] This figure reflects the system-wide cost of reducing peak demand by one kW — effectively the avoided cost of a capacity upgrade, diluted to account for the fact that most feeders are not constrained. RIE's planning engineers confirmed that per-project upgrade costs vary widely depending on local conditions, and that this avoided cost figure is the best available proxy for average marginal distribution capacity cost.
  - The result: marginal distribution capacity costs are concentrated in summer afternoons and are zero in winter — consistent with the feeder headroom analysis in Section III.

<br><br>

**Q.** Why did you not use the Non-Coincident Peak (NCP) method that RIE uses for class-level cost allocation to do your subclass cost allocation?
<br>
TODO: ADD OUR NCP-SUMMER RESULTS HERE.
**A.** Because the NCP method produces misleading results when applied at the subclass level.

  - The ACOSS NCP allocator assigns distribution costs to each class in proportion to its share of total non-coincident peak demand. This is a reasonable proxy at the class level, where it captures the intuition that classes with higher peaks impose more costs.
  - But at the subclass level — e.g., HP customers vs. non-HP customers within the residential class — NCP looks at each subgroup's peak demand magnitude without regard to **when** or **where** that peak occurs.
  - Heat pump customers peak in winter. But the grid has `{python} f"{f.median_headroom_winter:.0f}"`% median winter headroom and zero winter-constrained feeders. The NCP method would say HP customers are causing substantial winter costs; the actual grid data says they are not.
  - This sensitivity of NCP to how subgroups are defined was demonstrated in the Commission's own Docket 4770.
